_comment: lessons.json — thints language curriculum. Word data is pulled from dictionary.json at runtime using the IDs in wordIds. Only sentences, prompts, and structural metadata are hardcoded here.

units.title: Foundation
units.description: How the language is built — structure, the glue words, and your first sentences.

units.title: Time & Logic
units.description: Tense, time words, logical connectives, and how to modify and negate.

units.title: Vocabulary
units.description: Expanding your word bank across the core semantic groups.

units.title: Advanced Grammar
units.description: Modals, commands, subordinate clauses, evidentiality, and numbers.

units.title: Fluency
units.description: Putting it all together — complex sentences and borrowing words.

l01.title: How words are built
l01.subtitle: Suffixes, vowels, and the {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} family
l01.description: Every word in thints is built from a suffix (the topic family) and a vowel (which specific idea). Learn to read any word before you know it.

grammar.title: Word structure: suffix + vowel
grammar.body:
Every regular word is built from two pieces: a suffix and a vowel.

The suffix tells you the topic family — all words ending in {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} are about existence and state. The vowel tells you which specific idea within that family.

So {{dict:i:0001.word}} (alive) and {{dict:i:0010.word}} (life) are in the same family — they're both about existence — but different vowels pick different ideas.

grammar.title: 44 suffix families
grammar.body:
There are 44 suffix families in thints. When you hear an unfamiliar word, the suffix tells you the topic before you know the specific meaning.

Hear a word ending in {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}? You know it's about existence or state. Hear {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}}? You know it's a positive emotion. This makes the language highly decodable — even new words give you a clue.

practice.prompt: What does the suffix {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} tell you about a word?
options: It belongs to the existence & state family
practice.explanation: The suffix {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} marks the existence & state family. All words with this suffix deal with being, living, or existing.

practice.prompt: Which part of {{dict:i:0001.word}} tells you which specific idea it represents?
practice.explanation: The vowel {{dict:sv:𐑩.word}} picks the specific idea (alive/active) within the {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} family. Different vowels pick different ideas in the same family.

practice.prompt: {{dict:i:0010.word}} and {{dict:i:0001.word}} share the same suffix {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}. What does that tell you?
options: They are both about existence and state
practice.explanation: Same suffix means same family. {{dict:i:0010.word}} (life) and {{dict:i:0001.word}} (alive) are both in the existence & state family — different vowels, different ideas, same topic.

practice.prompt: Match each word to its meaning
You see a word ending in {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} that you have never seen before. What do you know about it before looking it up?
options: Nothing — you need to look it up
It is in the positive emotions family
It is a verb in past tense
practice.explanation: The {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} suffix marks the positive emotions family. Knowing suffixes lets you make an educated guess about any unfamiliar word.

l02.title: The glue words
l02.subtitle: Single-vowel reserved words
l02.description: Fourteen single-vowel words do enormous work in thints. They can take a prefix and be used like all other words. However, they have powerful functions on their own.

grammar.title: Single-vowel reserved words
grammar.body:
These words are each a single Shavian vowel. They are reserved — they cannot be modified with prefixes or suffixes. They function as-is.

Some are pronouns ({{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} = I, {{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} = you, {{dict:sv:𐑱.word}} = it/they, {{dict:sv:𐑵.word}} = we). Others are connectors ({{dict:sv:𐑧.word}} = and, {{dict:sv:𐑲.word}} = but, {{dict:sv:𐑨.word}} = or). Others are positional ({{dict:sv:𐑩.word}} = to/toward, {{dict:sv:𐑫.word}} = in, {{dict:sv:𐑦.word}} = if).

These are extremely high-frequency — ranks 2 through 15 in the dictionary. Mastering them gives you the skeleton of any sentence.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} is the first-person pronoun — I, me, myself, my.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:sv:𐑧.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:sv:𐑧.word}} means and, plus, also — it joins things together.

practice.prompt: You want to say 'you or I'. Which is correct?
practice.explanation: {{dict:sv:𐑨.word}} means or / either. {{dict:sv:𐑧.word}} means and. {{dict:sv:𐑲.word}} means but. {{dict:sv:𐑦.word}} means if.

practice.prompt: Match each reserved word to its meaning
Can you add a prefix to {{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} to change how it is used?
options: Yes — prefixes apply to all words
No — single-vowel reserved words never take prefixes
Yes — but only in formal writing
practice.explanation: Single-vowel reserved words are fixed. They cannot be modified with prefixes or suffixes — they work exactly as they are.

l03.title: Nouns and subjects
l03.subtitle: The subject prefix {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}
l03.description: Every root word in thints is POS-neutral — it is neither a noun nor a verb until a prefix is added. The prefix {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} marks the subject of a sentence.

grammar.title: Roots are POS-neutral
grammar.body:
In most languages, words have a fixed part of speech — 'run' is a verb, 'dog' is a noun. In thints, every root word is neutral. The prefix you attach determines whether it functions as a noun, verb, modifier, or something else.

This is one of the most important ideas in the language. The same root can play any role in a sentence depending on its prefix.

grammar.title: The subject prefix {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} marks the subject — who or what performs the action.

Attach {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} to any root and you have a subject noun. The prefix comes before the root with no space.

practice.prompt: What does the prefix {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} mark?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} marks the subject — who or what performs the action.

practice.prompt: {{dict:i:0002.word}} means 'someone'. How do you say 'someone (as the subject)'?
practice.explanation: Prefixes attach to the front of the root. {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} + {{dict:i:0002.word}} = {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}}.

practice.prompt: A thints root word without a prefix is...
options: POS-neutral — its role is undefined until a prefix is added
practice.explanation: Roots are POS-neutral. They need a prefix to have a grammatical role in a sentence.

practice.prompt: Match the root to its meaning
How do you say 'a person (as the subject)'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} (subject prefix) + {{dict:i:0087.word}} (person) = {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0087.word}}.

l04.title: Your first verbs
l04.subtitle: Present tense — prefix {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}
l04.description: The prefix {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} marks a verb in present tense. Combined with the subject prefix from L3, you can now make your first real sentences.

grammar.title: Present tense: prefix {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} marks a verb in present tense. The same root that was a subject noun with {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} becomes a verb with {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}.

This is the power of the prefix system — the root stays the same, the prefix changes the role.

grammar.title: Word order is mostly free
grammar.body:
In thints, word order is flexible. The prefixes tell you each word's role — you don't need position to know what is the subject and what is the verb.

The one constraint: modifiers must be placed directly next to what they modify. You'll learn this when we reach modifiers.

practice.prompt: What does the prefix {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} mark?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} marks a verb in present tense.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'knows' (present tense verb) using the root {{dict:i:0014.word}}?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} (present tense) + {{dict:i:0014.word}} (know) = {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}}. Prefixes always attach to the front.

practice.prompt: What is the difference between {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} and {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}}?
options: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} is past tense, {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} is present tense
{{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} is 'knowledge' as a subject noun, {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} is 'knows' as a verb
practice.explanation: Same root {{dict:i:0014.word}} (knowledge/knowing), different prefixes. {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} makes it a subject noun, {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} makes it a present-tense verb.

practice.prompt: What does {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} = I (subject), {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} = want (present verb). So: I want.

practice.prompt: Thints word order is...
options: Mostly free — prefixes tell you each word's role
practice.explanation: Word order is mostly free in thints. Because prefixes mark each word's grammatical role, position doesn't carry that information.

l05.title: Objects and indirect objects
l05.subtitle: Prefixes {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} and {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}}
l05.description: The same roots from L3 and L4 can now play new roles. {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} marks the direct object, {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} marks the indirect object.

grammar.title: Direct object: prefix {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} marks the direct object — who or what receives the action directly.

All noun prefixes use plosive stop sounds: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-generic-base-symbol}}. If you mishear one for another you still know it's a noun — just the role may be unclear.

grammar.title: Indirect object: prefix {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} marks the indirect object — who or what benefits from or receives the result of the action.

In English: 'I gave the dog a bone' — 'the dog' is the indirect object (receives the benefit), 'a bone' is the direct object (receives the action directly).

practice.prompt: {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} marks the...
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} marks the direct object — who or what receives the action directly.

practice.prompt: {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} marks the...
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} marks the indirect object — who or what benefits from the action.

practice.prompt: In 'I gave you this', which word is the indirect object?
practice.explanation: 'You' receives the benefit of the giving — that's the indirect object. 'This' is what was directly given — the direct object.

practice.prompt: What does {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} (direct object prefix) + {{dict:i:0002.word}} (someone) = 'someone' as the direct object.

practice.prompt: All noun prefixes ({{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}} {{prefix:noun-generic-base-symbol}}) share which acoustic quality?
options: They are all plosive stop sounds
practice.explanation: All noun prefixes use plosive stop sounds. Mishearing one for another still signals 'noun' — you may not know the exact role but you know it's a noun.

l06.title: Negation
l06.subtitle: The operator {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} and the word {{dict:i:0013.word}}
l06.description: Two ways to negate in thints: the operator {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} stacks onto prefix + root, and {{dict:i:0013.word}} is the standalone negation word.

grammar.title: The negation operator {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} is an operator — it stacks onto a base prefix to add negation. It always comes first in the stack.

Stacking order: negation ({{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}) + base role ({{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}/{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}/{{prefix:noun-indirect-base-symbol}}/{{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}…) + plural ({{prefix:operator-plural-symbol}} if applicable)

So {{prefix:noun-subject-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} means 'not someone (as subject)' — the person doing the action is specifically not that thing.

grammar.title: The negation word {{dict:i:0013.word}}
grammar.body:
{{dict:i:0013.word}} is a standalone word meaning 'not / no / negate'. It belongs to the Logic & Connectives family (suffix {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}}).

Use {{dict:i:0013.word}} when you want to negate a whole idea rather than a specific prefix. It is often more natural in spoken sentences.

practice.prompt: What does the operator {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} do?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} is the negation operator. It stacks before a base prefix: {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} + {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} = negated present verb, {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} + {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} = negated subject.

practice.prompt: What does {{prefix:verb-present-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} (negation) + {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} (present) + {{dict:i:0015.word}} (want) = does not want.

practice.prompt: What is the correct stacking order for a negated subject noun?
practice.explanation: Negation ({{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}) always comes first: {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} + base prefix + root.

practice.prompt: Match the word to its meaning
What is the difference between {{prefix:noun-subject-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} and {{dict:i:0013.word}} {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}}?
options: No difference — they mean the same thing

{{prefix:noun-subject-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} specifically negates the subject role; {{dict:i:0013.word}} {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} negates the idea more broadly
{{prefix:noun-subject-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} is a verb form, {{dict:i:0013.word}} {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} is a noun phrase
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} uses the operator to negate the specific subject role. {{dict:i:0013.word}} {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} uses the standalone negation word to broadly negate the phrase.

l07.title: Past and future
l07.subtitle: Prefixes {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}} and {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}
l07.description: Tense is marked entirely by the verb prefix. The root word never changes — only the prefix does.

grammar.title: The tense system: {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}} {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
Three prefixes cover all simple tenses:

{{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} — present: I know
{{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}} — past: I knew
{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} — future: I will know

All three use voiced nasals and continuants, so even if you mishear one, you know it's a verb in some tense.

grammar.title: Negated future: {{prefix:verb-future-negated-symbol}}
grammar.body:
The future tense uses {{prefix:operator-plural-symbol}} for negation rather than {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}. So 'will not' is {{prefix:verb-future-negated-symbol}}, not {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}.

This is one of the few irregular forms. In the future verb slot, {{prefix:operator-plural-symbol}} is repurposed as negation instead of plural. Context (verb position in the sentence) disambiguates.

practice.prompt: Which prefix marks past tense?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}} marks past tense. {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}} = present, {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} = future, {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} = ongoing/progressive.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I will want'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} (future) + {{dict:i:0015.word}} (want) = {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}}. With subject {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} (I): {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}}.

practice.prompt: How do you negate a future tense verb?
practice.explanation: Future negation uses {{prefix:verb-future-negated-symbol}} (not {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}). So 'will not know' = {{prefix:verb-future-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}}.

practice.prompt: Match the verb form to its tense
What does {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} = you (subject). {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} = wanted ({{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}} past + {{dict:i:0015.word}} want). So: you wanted.

l08.title: Time words
l08.subtitle: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} suffix family
l08.description: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} suffix family covers time — when things happen, how long they last, and their sequence.

grammar.title: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} time family
grammar.body:
All words ending in {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} deal with time in the abstract — when something happens, how long it lasts, and sequencing. These are different from clock time (that comes in the Time — Measurement & Cycles group).

Time words often appear without prefixes when used as sentence-level markers.

practice.prompt: What suffix family covers abstract time concepts?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}} is the time — abstract & sequence family.

practice.prompt: Match the time word to its meaning
What does {{dict:i:0026.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0026.word}} means duration, period, or span — how long something lasts.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0160.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0160.word}} means moment, instant, or a specific point in time.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'soon, I will speak'?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0042.word}} (soon) + {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} (I, subject) + {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0040.word}} (will speak, future). Word order is flexible, so {{dict:i:0042.word}} can come first.

l09.title: Logic and connectives
l09.subtitle: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} suffix family
l09.description: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} family handles logical relationships — of, not, because, condition, and more.

grammar.title: The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} logic family
grammar.body:
The {{prefix:verb-past-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} suffix family covers logical connectives — the words that link ideas. You already know {{dict:i:0013.word}} (not) from L6. This family includes of/between, yet/still, condition, because, and then.

These words often appear without prefixes, serving as sentence-level connectors.

practice.prompt: Match the logic word to its meaning
What does {{dict:i:0111.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0111.word}} means because, since, or for — it introduces a reason.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0168.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0168.word}} means then, next, or but — it marks sequence or contrast.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'I know, but I do not want'?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0011.word}} means yet / still / nevertheless — it introduces a contrast. {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} (I know) {{dict:i:0011.word}} (yet) {{prefix:verb-present-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} (do not want).

practice.prompt: {{dict:i:0005.word}} (of / between / relating) is used to show...
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0005.word}} shows a logical relationship — belonging, connection, or relevance between two things.

l10.title: Modifiers
l10.subtitle: Prefix {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} — adjectives and adverbs
l10.description: The prefix {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} turns any root into a modifier. Whether it reads as an adjective or adverb depends entirely on what it sits next to.

grammar.title: The modifier prefix {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} is the only modifier prefix. One prefix covers both adjectives and adverbs — position determines which reading applies.

{{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} before a noun = adjective ('big dog')
{{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} before a verb = adverb ('runs quickly')

The negated form is {{prefix:modifier-negated-symbol}}, not {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}{{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} — {{prefix:modifier-negated-symbol}} is its own dedicated sound for negated modifiers.

grammar.title: Modifier adjacency rule
grammar.body:
The one hard word-order rule in thints: modifiers must be placed directly next to the word they modify.

This is enforced because without it, {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0027.word}} could modify anything in the sentence and the meaning would be ambiguous.

practice.prompt: What prefix marks a modifier (adjective or adverb)?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} is the modifier prefix. Same prefix for both adjectives and adverbs — position determines which.

practice.prompt: {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0027.word}} placed before a noun reads as...
practice.explanation: {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} before a noun = adjective. {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} before a verb = adverb. Same word, position decides.

practice.prompt: How do you form a negated modifier (e.g. 'not big')?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:modifier-negated-symbol}} is the dedicated negated modifier prefix. It is not {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} stacked onto {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}} — {{prefix:modifier-negated-symbol}} is its own distinct sound.

practice.prompt: Match the root to its meaning
Why must modifiers be adjacent to what they modify?
options: Without it, the modifier's target is ambiguous since word order is otherwise free
practice.explanation: Because thints word order is otherwise free, the only way to know what a modifier modifies is its physical position next to the target.

l11.title: Ongoing action
l11.subtitle: Progressive aspect — prefix {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}
l11.description: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} marks ongoing or continuous action. It covers the present progressive and is the most common aspect marker.

grammar.title: Progressive aspect: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
{{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} marks an ongoing or continuous action — 'I am knowing' / 'I am wanting'. It is the present progressive by default.

{{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} alone covers the most common case. Past and future progressive use the ticked sounds ({{prefix:verb-progressive-past-ticked-symbol}} and {{prefix:verb-progressive-future-ticked-symbol}}) which you'll learn later.

examples.gloss: I am knowing / I am getting to know

practice.prompt: What does the prefix {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} mark?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} marks progressive aspect — ongoing or continuous action.

practice.prompt: What is the difference between {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} and {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}}?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} = knows (simple present). {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} = is knowing / in the process of knowing (progressive).

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I am not speaking'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-progressive-negated-symbol}} is the negated progressive (not ongoing). {{prefix:verb-progressive-negated-symbol}} + {{dict:i:0040.word}} (speak) = {{prefix:verb-progressive-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0040.word}} (is not speaking).

practice.prompt: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} alone defaults to which tense?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}} alone = present progressive ('I am doing'). Past and future progressive use ticked sounds covered later.

practice.prompt: What does {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} mean?
options: someone is wanting / someone is in a state of wanting
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-progressive-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0015.word}} = is wanting (ongoing present progressive). With {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0002.word}} (someone, subject): someone is wanting.

l12.title: Quantity and possession
l12.subtitle: Prefix {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}
l12.description: {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} marks quantity (how many) when placed before a number, and possession (belonging to) when placed before a pronoun.

grammar.title: Prefix {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}: quantity and possession
grammar.body:
{{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} does double duty:

Before a number: quantity — {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}3 {{dict:i:0002.word}} = 3 somethings
Before a pronoun: possession — {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} {{dict:i:0002.word}} = my something

Context determines which reading applies — a digit after {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} means quantity; a pronoun after {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} means possession.

practice.prompt: What does {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} + a pronoun express?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} before a pronoun marks possession. {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} = my, {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} = your, {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑵.word}} = our.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'your thing'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} = your ({{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} possession + {{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} you). {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑴.word}} {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0004.word}} = your thing.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0072.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0072.word}} = each, every, all — universal quantity.

practice.prompt: Match the quantity/logic word to its meaning
{{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} before a number vs {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} before a pronoun — what is the difference?
options: Before a number = quantity; before a pronoun = possession
Before a number = possession; before a pronoun = quantity
practice.explanation: {{prefix:quantity-base-symbol}} is context-sensitive: before a digit it means quantity, before a pronoun it means possession.

l13.title: Relating things
l13.subtitle: Relation & Causation — the {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} family
l13.description: Words about how things relate to each other — belonging, similarity, cause, and result.

grammar.title: The {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} relation family
grammar.body: The {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}} suffix family covers how things relate — of/belonging, similar/like, solution/means, and cause/result. These words are frequently used as connectors in complex sentences.

practice.prompt: Match the relation word to its meaning
What does {{dict:i:0033.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0033.word}} = similar, like, comparable — it marks similarity or analogy.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'this is like that'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0004.word}} (this, subject) + {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0033.word}} (is similar, present verb using root {{dict:i:0033.word}}) + {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0004.word}} (that, object).

practice.prompt: {{dict:i:0028.word}} expresses...
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0028.word}} covers belonging to, of, origin, and causation — the most general relation word.

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0057.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0057.word}} = solution, means, method — the way something is achieved.

l14.title: Quality and value
l14.subtitle: Evaluation & Morality — the {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} family
l14.description: Words about quality, standards, and judgment — from ok to outstanding, from value to wrongness.

grammar.title: The {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} evaluation family
grammar.body:
The {{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}}{{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} suffix family covers evaluation and moral judgment — how good, how appropriate, how valuable something is.

This family runs from ok/acceptable through great/excellent. The vowel marks the degree and valence.

practice.prompt: Match the evaluation word to its meaning
What does {{dict:i:0222.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0222.word}} = valuable, expensive, precious — high worth.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'this is great'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0004.word}} (this, subject) + {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0078.word}} (is great, present verb from root {{dict:i:0078.word}}).

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0166.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0166.word}} = fair, equal, just — balanced evaluation.

practice.prompt: {{dict:i:0127.word}} means...
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0127.word}} = quality, standard, grade — the level or measure of something's worth.

l15.title: Positive emotions
l15.subtitle: The {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} family
l15.description: All positive emotions end in {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}}. From delight to love to happiness — recognizing this suffix means you always know you're in emotional territory.

practice.prompt: Match the emotion to its meaning
What does {{dict:i:0125.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0125.word}} = cute, charming, lovely — appealing in a soft way.

practice.prompt: How would you say 'I am happy'?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:noun-subject-base-symbol}}{{dict:sv:𐑰.word}} (I, subject) + {{prefix:verb-present-base-symbol}}{{dict:i:0051.word}} (am happy, present verb from root {{dict:i:0051.word}}).

practice.prompt: What does {{dict:i:0288.word}} mean?
practice.explanation: {{dict:i:0288.word}} = sweet, pleasant, delightful.

practice.prompt: What does the {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} suffix tell you about a word before you know which one it is?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} is the positive emotions suffix. Any word ending in {{prefix:verb-future-base-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} is in that family.

l16.title: Negative emotions
l16.subtitle: Negative emotion words
l16.description: The counterparts to L15 — negative emotions and their vocabulary.

l17.title: Motion
l17.subtitle: Motion & Movement — the {{prefix:modifier-base-symbol}}{{prefix:noun-object-base-symbol}} family
l17.description: Words about how things move — direction, speed, travel.

l18.title: Place and space
l18.subtitle: Place & Dwelling + Axes & Dimensions
l18.description: Spatial language — where things are and how they relate in space.

l19.title: The body
l19.subtitle: Body — Basic Parts
l19.description: Core vocabulary for the human body.

l20.title: Social interaction
l20.subtitle: Social Interaction vocabulary
l20.description: Greetings, farewells, and the words that make social exchange work.

l21.title: Work and effort
l21.subtitle: Work, Effort & Outcome vocabulary
l21.description: Doing things, trying, succeeding, and failing.

l22.title: Nature
l22.subtitle: Nature & Elements vocabulary
l22.description: The natural world — elements, living things, and natural phenomena.

l23.title: Can, should, would
l23.subtitle: Modal prefixes {{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}}
l23.description: Modals express ability, obligation, and conditionality. They use voiced obstruents — acoustically verb-adjacent but distinct.

grammar.title: Modal prefixes: {{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}}
grammar.body:
Three modal prefixes:

{{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} — can / could (ability)
{{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}} — should / must (obligation)
{{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}} — would / conditional

Negating ability ({{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}}) and obligation ({{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}}) uses a negative modifier word before the modal, not a stacked {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}} prefix. Only conditional ({{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}}) takes a stacked {{prefix:operator-plural-symbol}}: {{prefix:modal-conditional-negated-symbol}} = would not.

practice.prompt: Which prefix means 'can / could' (ability)?
practice.explanation: {{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} = can / could. {{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}} = should / must. {{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}} = would / conditional.

practice.prompt: How do you say 'I would not know'?
practice.explanation: Conditional negation uses {{prefix:modal-conditional-negated-symbol}}, not {{prefix:operator-negation-symbol}}{{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}}. {{prefix:modal-conditional-negated-symbol}}{{dict:i:0014.word}} = would not know.

practice.prompt: To say 'I cannot speak', you use...
options: A negative modifier word before {{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} + root
practice.explanation: Ability ({{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}}) and obligation ({{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}}) negate with a modifier word before them, not a stacked prefix. {{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}} is the only modal that takes a stacked negation ({{prefix:modal-conditional-negated-symbol}}).

practice.prompt: Match the modal to its meaning
What acoustic quality do modal prefixes {{prefix:modal-ability-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-obligation-base-symbol}} {{prefix:modal-conditional-base-symbol}} share?
practice.explanation: Modal prefixes use voiced obstruents — acoustically verb-adjacent but distinct enough to be recognizable as modals.

l24.title: Commands
l24.subtitle: Do! and Do not! — prefixes {{prefix:verb-command-do-symbol}} and {{prefix:verb-command-do-not-symbol}}
l24.description: The imperative — telling someone to do or not do something. Phonetically distinct from all other verb prefixes.

l25.title: Subordinate clauses
l25.subtitle: Clause verb prefix {{prefix:verb-clause-base-symbol}}
l25.description: How to embed one clause inside another — 'I know that you want...'

l26.title: Reported speech
l26.subtitle: The evidential {{prefix:evidential-reportative-base-symbol}}
l26.description: Marking whether you witnessed something directly or are reporting what you heard.

l27.title: Was doing, will be doing
l27.subtitle: Ticked progressive: {{prefix:verb-progressive-past-ticked-symbol}} {{prefix:verb-progressive-future-ticked-symbol}} {{prefix:verb-progressive-emphatic-ticked-symbol}}
l27.description: Extending the progressive to past and future tenses using ticked consonants.

l28.title: Numbers
l28.subtitle: Sequence markers {{prefix:number-integer-end-base-symbol}} {{prefix:number-decimal-start-base-symbol}} {{prefix:number-fraction-base-symbol}} {{prefix:number-scale-up-symbol}} {{prefix:number-scale-down-symbol}}
l28.description: How numbers work in thints — sequence boundaries, decimals, fractions, and scale.

l29.title: Complex sentences
l29.subtitle: Putting it all together
l29.description: Combining everything — free word order, modifier adjacency, multiple clauses, all tenses.

l30.title: Borrowed words
l30.subtitle: Alias roots and {{prefix:noun-generic-base-symbol}}
l30.description: How to bring words from other languages into thints — alias roots, the generic noun prefix, and conversation handles.
